# Machine Learning

In trading research, machine learning is not a single method but a family of
problem formulations. The distinction matters because the choice of learning
paradigm determines what the model is asked to do. A model may be trained to
forecast a return, classify a market condition, discover latent structure in
the data, or choose actions that directly affect portfolio evolution.

For this thesis, the role of the present chapter is not to review machine
learning in general terms. Instead, it is to position reinforcement learning
relative to other paradigms that are commonly used in quantitative trading.
This comparison is necessary because trading can be formulated either as a
prediction task or as a sequential control problem, and those formulations lead
to materially different modeling choices.

In the sections that follow, supervised learning is treated as the framework
most naturally suited to forecasting labeled targets such as future returns or
directional classes. Unsupervised learning is treated as a tool for extracting
structure from market data when explicit labels are unavailable. Semi-supervised
learning occupies an intermediate position in which a limited set of labeled
examples is combined with a larger unlabeled sample.

The present thesis focuses on reinforcement learning because the empirical task
is not only to predict the market, but to determine a sequence of portfolio
exposure decisions under uncertainty, transaction frictions, and delayed
performance feedback. The following subsections therefore compare the main
learning paradigms in trading contexts before the reinforcement-learning
foundations are developed in the next chapter.
